question.text: The distribution waterfall section in the PPM of Krishna Private Equity Fund specifies the order in which proceeds are distributed. What is the typical sequence in a standard European-style waterfall?

answer.text: Performance fee first, then return of capital, then preferred return
Equal distribution between manager and investors from the first rupee of profit
Return of contributed capital first, then preferred return to investors, then carried interest/performance fee to the manager with a catch-up provision
All proceeds go to the manager until management fees are recovered, then to investors

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A European-style (whole-fund) waterfall prioritizes: (1) return of all contributed capital to investors, (2) preferred return (hurdle rate) to investors, (3) catch-up to the manager, and (4) carried interest split. This protects investors by ensuring capital return and minimum return before the manager earns performance compensation. **The Trap:** The first option reverses the priority, which would heavily favor the manager — no sophisticated investor would agree to such terms. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series XIX-B tests waterfall structures from the fund manager's fee calculation perspective. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Understand whether the fund uses a European (whole-fund) or American (deal-by-deal) waterfall. European waterfalls are more investor-friendly as the manager earns carry only after all capital is returned.
